title: Performance Optimization
description: Master GPU performance analysis through memory coalescing, the roofline model for identifying bottlenecks, and systematic profiling techniques to iteratively optimize your CUDA kernels.

sections.title: Coalescing at the Hardware Level
sections.content:
If you only learn one GPU optimization technique, let it be **memory coalescing**. The difference between coalesced and uncoalesced memory access can be **10-20x** in performance. No amount of occupancy tuning (Module 5) or divergence elimination will save a kernel with terrible memory access patterns. This module ties together everything from Modules 2-5. Now that you understand threads (Module 2), shared memory (Module 3), tiling (Module 4), and warps (Module 5), you can analyze and optimize any kernel. This is where it all comes together. Global memory (GPU DRAM) is accessed in **transactions** of 32 or 128 bytes. When threads in a warp (the 32-thread groups from Module 5) access memory, the hardware attempts to **combine (coalesce)** their requests into the minimum number of transactions. **Coalesced access**: All 32 threads in a warp access consecutive memory addresses. The hardware combines this into a single 128-byte transaction. One trip to memory serves all 32 threads. **Uncoalesced access**: Threads access scattered or strided addresses. Each thread's request may require a separate transaction. On modern GPUs (Pascal and later) the hardware uses 32-byte sectors, so worst case is roughly 8x bandwidth waste for 4-byte loads (32 sectors of 32 bytes for 128 bytes of useful data); on older architectures with strict 128-byte transactions the worst case was 32x. Either way, the penalty is severe. Think of it like a library. Coalesced access is like requesting pages 1-32 of a book -- the librarian grabs one volume. Uncoalesced access is like requesting page 1 from book A, page 47 from book B, page 203 from book C -- the librarian makes many separate trips. Global memory is divided into **128-byte segments** (also called cache lines). When a warp issues a memory request: 1. The hardware collects all 32 thread addresses 2. It determines which 128-byte segments are touched (and which 32-byte sectors within them) 3. It issues one transaction per sector touched **Best case** (perfectly coalesced): All 32 threads access consecutive `float32` values (32 x 4 bytes = 128 bytes). One segment, one transaction. **Worst case** (fully strided): Each thread accesses a different segment. 32 sectors, 32 transactions. The same useful data, many times more bandwidth consumed. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph layout["Memory layout (128-byte segment)"] direction LR M0["f0
0x000"] ~~~ M1["f1
0x004"] ~~~ M2["f2
0x008"] ~~~ Me["..."] ~~~ M31["f31
0x07C"] end subgraph coal["COALESCED (stride=1)"] CT["32 threads -> 32 consecutive floats
All in same 128B segment
= 1 transaction"] end subgraph stride["STRIDED (stride=1024)"] ST["T0->f0, T1->f1024, T2->f2048, ...
Each in DIFFERENT segment
= 32 transactions (waste)"] end subgraph rnd["RANDOM"] RT["Each thread -> random segment
Worst case: 32 different segments
= 32 transactions"] end classDef good fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca class CT good class ST,RT bad ```

sections.title: Row vs Column-Major
sections.content:
Consider a 2D matrix stored in **row-major** order (C/numpy default), where element `M[row][col]` is at memory address `base + row * num_cols + col`.

```python
# Row-major layout in memory:
# M[0][0], M[0][1], M[0][2], ..., M[0][N-1],  <- Row 0
# M[1][0], M[1][1], M[1][2], ..., M[1][N-1],  <- Row 1
# ...
```

**Coalesced pattern** -- threads read along a row:

```python
@cuda.jit
def read_row(matrix, output, rows, cols):
    row = cuda.blockIdx.x
    col = cuda.threadIdx.x  # Consecutive threads -> consecutive columns
    if row < rows and col < cols:
        output[row * cols + col] = matrix[row, col]
    # Thread 0 reads M[row][0], Thread 1 reads M[row][1], ...
    # These are consecutive in memory -> COALESCED!
```

**Uncoalesced pattern** -- threads read along a column:

```python
@cuda.jit
def read_col(matrix, output, rows, cols):
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x
    row = cuda.threadIdx.x  # Consecutive threads -> consecutive rows
    if row < rows and col < cols:
        output[row * cols + col] = matrix[row, col]
    # Thread 0 reads M[0][col], Thread 1 reads M[1][col], ...
    # These are COLS apart in memory -> UNCOALESCED!
    # Stride = cols * 4 bytes. If cols=1024, stride=4096 bytes.
    # Each thread hits a different 128-byte segment.
```

The performance difference is dramatic:

| Access Pattern | Transactions per warp | Relative Performance |
|---------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Row-wise (coalesced) | 1 | 1.0x (baseline) |
| Column-wise (stride = N) | up to 32 | 0.05x - 0.1x |
| Random | up to 32 | 0.03x - 0.1x |

Let us calculate the bandwidth waste. On a T4 GPU with ~320 GB/s memory bandwidth:

**Coalesced**: Reading 1M float32 values = 4 MB. At 320 GB/s, this takes ~13 microseconds.

**Strided (stride = 1024)**: Each warp reads 32 values (128 bytes of useful data) but triggers many sector transactions, transferring 4 KB or more of bytes for the same 128 bytes of useful data. The effective bandwidth drops by an order of magnitude. The same 4 MB read can take 10-30x longer.

This is not a theoretical exercise. Uncoalesced access is the most common performance bug in GPU code.

sections.title: Restructuring Techniques
sections.content:
### Technique 1: Transpose Your Thinking

If your algorithm naturally processes columns, transpose the matrix first:

```python
# Instead of processing columns of a row-major matrix (bad):
# Process rows of the transposed matrix (good)!
matrix_T = matrix.T.copy()  # Transpose on CPU
d_matrix_T = cuda.to_device(matrix_T)
# Now row access on the transpose = column access on the original
# But the memory access is coalesced!
```

The transpose costs O(N^2) but is a one-time cost. If you process the matrix many times, the coalesced access saves far more than the transpose costs.

### Technique 2: Structure of Arrays (SoA) vs Array of Structures (AoS)

This is one of the most important data layout decisions in GPU programming:

```python
# Array of Structures (AoS) -- BAD for GPU coalescing
# particles = [(x0,y0,z0,m0), (x1,y1,z1,m1), (x2,y2,z2,m2), ...]
# Memory: x0,y0,z0,m0, x1,y1,z1,m1, x2,y2,z2,m2, ...
# If threads read all x-values: stride of 4 floats between each x -> uncoalesced

# Structure of Arrays (SoA) -- GOOD for GPU coalescing
# x = [x0, x1, x2, ...]
# y = [y0, y1, y2, ...]
# z = [z0, z1, z2, ...]
# m = [m0, m1, m2, ...]
# If threads read all x-values: consecutive in memory -> coalesced!
```

On CPUs, AoS is often preferred because it keeps related data in the same cache line (good for processing one particle at a time). On GPUs, SoA is almost always better because adjacent threads process adjacent elements, and you want those elements consecutive in memory.

### Technique 3: Shared Memory as a Coalescing Buffer

When your algorithm requires non-coalesced access patterns, use shared memory as an intermediary:

1. Load data from global memory with **coalesced** access into shared memory
2. Rearrange the data in shared memory (fast, no coalescing needed)
3. Process from shared memory in whatever order your algorithm requires

```python
@cuda.jit
def transpose_tiled(input_matrix, output_matrix, rows, cols):
    TILE = 32
    tile = cuda.shared.array((TILE, TILE), dtype=float32)
    
    # Coalesced read from input
    x = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE + cuda.threadIdx.x
    y = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE + cuda.threadIdx.y
    if x < cols and y < rows:
        tile[cuda.threadIdx.y, cuda.threadIdx.x] = input_matrix[y, x]
    
    cuda.syncthreads()
    
    # Coalesced write to output (transposed indices)
    x = cuda.blockIdx.y * TILE + cuda.threadIdx.x
    y = cuda.blockIdx.x * TILE + cuda.threadIdx.y
    if x < rows and y < cols:
        output_matrix[y, x] = tile[cuda.threadIdx.x, cuda.threadIdx.y]
```

Both the read and write are coalesced (consecutive threads access consecutive elements), even though we are performing a transpose.

!pip install numba

from numba import cuda
import numpy as np
import math
import time

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment: Coalesced vs Strided Memory Access
# ------------------------------------------------------------------

# Kernel: coalesced row-wise read of a 2D matrix
@cuda.jit
def read_coalesced(matrix, output, rows, cols):
    """Each thread reads one element; consecutive threads read consecutive columns."""
    row = cuda.blockIdx.y * cuda.blockDim.y + cuda.threadIdx.y
    col = cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x + cuda.threadIdx.x
    if row < rows and col < cols:
        # Consecutive threads (varying threadIdx.x) read consecutive cols
        # -> consecutive memory addresses -> COALESCED
        output[row * cols + col] = matrix[row, col] * 2.0

# Kernel: uncoalesced column-wise read of a 2D matrix
@cuda.jit
def read_strided(matrix, output, rows, cols):
    """Each thread reads one element; consecutive threads read consecutive rows."""
    col = cuda.blockIdx.y * cuda.blockDim.y + cuda.threadIdx.y
    row = cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x + cuda.threadIdx.x
    if row < rows and col < cols:
        # Consecutive threads (varying threadIdx.x) read consecutive rows
        # -> addresses are 'cols' apart -> STRIDED -> UNCOALESCED
        output[row * cols + col] = matrix[row, col] * 2.0

def bench_kernel(kernel, args, grid, block, warmup=10, iters=50):
    for _ in range(warmup):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    return (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters * 1000  # ms

# Setup: large matrix
rows, cols = 4096, 4096
matrix = np.random.randn(rows, cols).astype(np.float32)
d_matrix = cuda.to_device(matrix)
d_output = cuda.device_array(rows * cols, dtype=np.float32)

# Block (16, 16) matches the convention used in Module 2.
# Note: with (16, 16), each warp spans 2 rows of the block (32/16 = 2).
# Coalescing still works because consecutive threadIdx.x values map to
# consecutive columns of a row-major matrix.
BLOCK = (16, 16)
grid_coal = (math.ceil(cols / BLOCK[0]), math.ceil(rows / BLOCK[1]))

# Block and grid for strided (x = row, y = col)
grid_stri = (math.ceil(rows / BLOCK[0]), math.ceil(cols / BLOCK[1]))

data_bytes = rows * cols * 4  # float32

t_coal = bench_kernel(read_coalesced, (d_matrix, d_output, rows, cols), grid_coal, BLOCK)
t_stri = bench_kernel(read_strided, (d_matrix, d_output, rows, cols), grid_stri, BLOCK)

bw_coal = (data_bytes * 2) / (t_coal / 1000) / 1e9  # GB/s (read + write)
bw_stri = (data_bytes * 2) / (t_stri / 1000) / 1e9

print("=== Memory Coalescing Experiment ===")
print(f"Matrix: {rows} x {cols} float32 ({data_bytes / 1e6:.1f} MB)\n")
print(f"{'Pattern':<25} {'Time (ms)':<14} {'Bandwidth (GB/s)':<20} {'Relative'}")
print("-" * 70)
print(f"{'Coalesced (row-wise)':<25} {t_coal:<14.3f} {bw_coal:<20.1f} {'1.00x (baseline)'}")
print(f"{'Strided (col-wise)':<25} {t_stri:<14.3f} {bw_stri:<20.1f} {t_stri/t_coal:.1f}x slower")
print(f"\nBandwidth utilization:")
print(f"  Coalesced: {bw_coal:.1f} GB/s")
print(f"  Strided:   {bw_stri:.1f} GB/s")
print(f"  Efficiency ratio: {bw_stri/bw_coal*100:.1f}%")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 2: Stride size impact
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
@cuda.jit
def read_with_stride(data, output, n, stride):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        src_idx = (idx * stride) % n  # Wrap around
        output[idx] = data[src_idx] * 2.0

n = 2_000_000
d_data = cuda.to_device(np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32))
d_out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
TPB = 256
BPG = math.ceil(n / TPB)

print("\n=== Stride Size vs Performance ===")
print(f"{'Stride':<10} {'Time (ms)':<14} {'Effective BW (GB/s)':<22} {'Slowdown'}")
print("-" * 60)

baseline = None
for stride in [1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128]:
    t = bench_kernel(read_with_stride, (d_data, d_out, n, stride), BPG, TPB)
    bw = (n * 4 * 2) / (t / 1000) / 1e9
    if baseline is None:
        baseline = t
    label = "(coalesced)" if stride == 1 else ""
    print(f"{stride:<10} {t:<14.4f} {bw:<22.1f} {t/baseline:.2f}x {label}")

sections.title: Mistakes & Real Numbers
sections.content:
## Common Coalescing Mistakes | Mistake | Why It Happens | Fix | |---------|---------------|-----| | Column access on row-major matrix | Natural for some algorithms | Transpose or use shared memory tiling | | Accessing every Nth element | Strided algorithms | Restructure to consecutive access | | AoS data layout | Habit from CPU programming | Convert to SoA | | Random access (hash tables, graphs) | Algorithmic requirement | Use shared memory, texture cache, or sort data | ## Think Like a GPU: Your Kernel Does a Matrix Transpose Let us trace through what happens at the warp level during a naive matrix transpose. You are Warp 0 in a block transposing a 1024x1024 matrix; your 32 threads have col indices 0-31 and row = 0. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph rd["READ: input[0, 0..31]"] R["32 threads read consecutive columns of row 0
-> consecutive in memory
= 1 transaction (COALESCED)"] end subgraph wr["WRITE: output[0..31, 0]"] W["32 threads write consecutive rows of column 0
-> stride 1024 floats
= 32 transactions (UNCOALESCED!)"] end Fix["FIX: Use shared memory tile as buffer
1. Coalesced read -> shmem
2. Any pattern in shmem (no coalescing rules)
3. Coalesced write from shmem"] rd --> Fix wr --> Fix classDef good fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 classDef bad fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca class R good class W bad ``` The FIX is exactly the tiling technique from Module 4: read a tile coalesced into shared memory, then write from shared memory in a coalesced pattern. > **Quick check:** In the tiled matmul from Module 4, is the load of A coalesced? Is the load of B coalesced? (Answer: Loading A -- threads in the same warp vary in tx, reading A[row, phase*T + tx]. Consecutive tx means consecutive columns, which is consecutive in row-major memory -- COALESCED. Loading B -- threads vary in tx, reading B[phase*T + ty, col]. Consecutive tx means consecutive col, consecutive in memory -- also COALESCED. The tiled matmul has good coalescing by design.) > **Gotcha:** A common mistake is to swap the x and y thread indices when computing row and col from cuda.grid(2). Writing `row, col = cuda.grid(2)` instead of `col, row = cuda.grid(2)` makes consecutive threads access consecutive rows instead of columns, turning coalesced access into strided access. This single line swap can cause a 10x slowdown. ## Real Performance Numbers: Coalescing Impact on T4 | Pattern | Time (ms) | Bandwidth | Slowdown | |---------|-----------|-----------|----------| | Coalesced (stride=1) | 0.42 | 305 GB/s | 1.0x | | Stride=2 | 0.68 | 189 GB/s | 1.6x | | Stride=4 | 1.01 | 127 GB/s | 2.4x | | Stride=16 | 2.85 | 45 GB/s | 6.8x | | Stride=32 | 4.92 | 26 GB/s | 11.7x | | Stride=128 | 5.31 | 24 GB/s | 12.6x | | Random | 5.89 | 22 GB/s | 14.0x | *4096x4096 float32 matrix, read + multiply + write. Peak bandwidth: 320 GB/s.* ## Key Takeaways 1. **Coalesced access**: consecutive threads access consecutive addresses -- the hardware combines into 1-4 transactions 2. **Uncoalesced access**: strided or random patterns can require many more transactions (up to 32 sectors of wasted bandwidth) 3. **Row access on row-major data is coalesced**; column access is not 4. **Structure of Arrays (SoA)** layout is almost always better than AoS for GPUs 5. **Shared memory** can serve as a coalescing buffer for algorithms requiring non-sequential access (the same tiling technique from Module 4) 6. **Now you can analyze any kernel**: identify the memory access pattern, check if consecutive threads access consecutive addresses, and fix it if they do not

sections.title: Why Some Kernels Are Fast
sections.content:
You have learned about memory coalescing (sections 1-4), warp divergence (Module 5), and occupancy (Module 5). But how do you know which one matters more for **your specific kernel**? The answer lies in a concept called **arithmetic intensity** and a visual tool called the **roofline model**.

This is the unifying framework for everything in the course. Once you understand the roofline model, you can look at any kernel and immediately know what to optimize.

Arithmetic intensity (AI) is the ratio of floating-point operations (FLOPS) to bytes transferred from memory:

```
Arithmetic Intensity = FLOPS / Bytes Transferred
```

A kernel that performs 100 floating-point operations but loads 400 bytes of data has an AI of 100/400 = **0.25 FLOPS/byte**. This is a **memory-bound** kernel -- its performance is limited by how fast it can feed data to the compute units.

A kernel that performs 10,000 floating-point operations on 400 bytes of data has an AI of 10,000/400 = **25 FLOPS/byte**. This is a **compute-bound** kernel -- its performance is limited by the raw compute throughput of the GPU.

### Example: Vector Addition

```python
@cuda.jit
def vector_add(a, b, c, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]  # 1 FLOP, 12 bytes (2 reads + 1 write, float32)
```

- FLOPS per element: 1 (the addition)
- Bytes per element: 12 (read `a[idx]` = 4B, read `b[idx]` = 4B, write `c[idx]` = 4B)
- AI = 1/12 ~ **0.083 FLOPS/byte** -- extremely memory-bound!

### Example: Matrix Multiplication

```python
# For N x N matrices:
# Each output element requires N multiply-adds = 2N FLOPS
# Total FLOPS: N^2 * 2N = 2N^3
# Total data: 3 matrices * N^2 * 4 bytes = 12N^2 bytes
# AI = 2N^3 / 12N^2 = N/6 FLOPS/byte
```

For N=1024: AI = 1024/6 ~ **170 FLOPS/byte** -- compute-bound!

This is why matrix multiplication scales so well on GPUs and vector addition does not. Matrix multiply reuses each loaded element N times, amortizing the memory cost.

> **Quick check:** What is the arithmetic intensity of the tiled matmul from Module 4 with TILE_SIZE=16? (Answer: Tiling does not change the total FLOPS -- still 2N^3. But it changes the effective bytes transferred: each element is loaded from global memory ceil(K/16) times per block instead of K times. The effective AI increases by TILE_SIZE: from N/6 to TILE_SIZE * N/6. But more practically, per-element it is TILE_SIZE/4 = 4 FLOPS/byte. Tiling moved us from 0.25 to 4 FLOPS/byte.)

### Where Common Kernels Sit

| Kernel | FLOPS/element | Bytes/element | AI (FLOPS/byte) | Bound |
|--------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|-------|
| Vector add | 1 | 12 | 0.083 | Memory |
| Vector multiply-add | 2 | 16 | 0.125 | Memory |
| Dot product (per elem) | 2 | 8 | 0.25 | Memory |
| Reduction (sum) | 1 | 4 | 0.25 | Memory |
| Stencil (5-point) | 5 | 4 (with reuse) | 1.25 | Memory |
| Naive matmul (per elem) | 2K | 8K | 0.25 | Memory |
| Tiled matmul (TILE=16) | 2K | 512 (8K/16) | 4.0 | Memory |
| Convolution (7x7 filter) | 98 | 4 | 24.5 | Balanced |
| Matrix multiply (N=1024) | 2048 | 12 | ~170 | Compute |

sections.title: The Roofline Model
sections.content:
The roofline model is a visual tool that shows the **maximum achievable performance** of a kernel given its arithmetic intensity and the hardware capabilities.

The model has two components:

1. **Memory bandwidth ceiling**: A sloped line representing `Performance = AI x Bandwidth`. For memory-bound kernels, performance grows linearly with AI.

2. **Compute throughput ceiling**: A flat line representing the peak FLOPS of the GPU.

The **roofline** is the minimum of these two:

```
Attainable Performance = min(Peak_FLOPS, AI x Peak_Bandwidth)
```

The **ridge point** is where the two lines meet:

```
Ridge Point AI = Peak_FLOPS / Peak_Bandwidth
```

For a T4 GPU:
- Peak FP32 compute: ~8.1 TFLOPS
- Peak memory bandwidth: ~320 GB/s
- Ridge point: 8100 / 320 = **25.3 FLOPS/byte**

Kernels with AI < 25.3 are memory-bound. Kernels with AI > 25.3 are compute-bound.

The roofline plot below shows where typical T4 kernels sit (log-log axes):

```cudadiagram
Roofline
```

**Where do our kernels sit on the T4?**

| Kernel | AI | Max GFLOPS | % of peak compute |
|--------|----|----|-------------------|
| Vec add | 0.083 | 27 | 0.3% |
| Reduction | 0.25 | 80 | 1.0% |
| Tiled matmul | 4.0 | 1280 | 16% |
| Conv 7x7 | 24.5 | 7840 | 97% |
| MatMul N=1024 | 170 | 8100 | 100% |

### Think Like a GPU: Your Kernel Does 2 FLOPs per 8 Bytes Loaded

Let us say you have written a dot product kernel. Each element performs 1 multiply + 1 add = 2 FLOPs, reading 2 floats = 8 bytes. AI = 2/8 = 0.25 FLOPS/byte.

On the roofline, max performance = 0.25 * 320 GB/s = 80 GFLOPS. T4 peak compute is 8100 GFLOPS, so you can use at most 80/8100 ~ 1% of the GPU's compute power.

What should you optimize?
- **Coalescing? YES** -- you are memory-bound, so every byte of bandwidth counts. Going from 50% to 90% coalescing efficiency nearly doubles your performance.
- **Divergence? NO** -- even perfect divergence only helps compute throughput, but you are not compute-limited.
- **Occupancy? MAYBE** -- higher occupancy helps hide memory latency, but only up to the bandwidth limit.

How to improve? Fuse with another operation (increase FLOPS), cache partial results in shared memory (decrease bytes), or restructure the algorithm for more data reuse (this is exactly what tiling does for matmul -- Module 4).

To use the roofline model:

1. **Calculate your kernel's AI** -- count FLOPS and bytes per element
2. **Plot it on the x-axis** of the roofline chart
3. **Read up to the roofline** -- that is your theoretical maximum performance
4. **Compare to actual performance** -- the gap shows optimization potential

If your kernel is **below the memory bandwidth roof**: you are memory-bound. Optimize memory access patterns (coalescing, caching), consider algorithmic changes to increase reuse. Improving compute will NOT help.

If your kernel is **below the compute roof**: you are compute-bound. Optimize instruction mix (use faster operations, FMA), increase occupancy for better latency hiding. Memory optimizations will NOT help.

sections.title: AI Calculation in Practice
sections.content:
Here is how to calculate AI for a real kernel:

```python
@cuda.jit
def saxpy(a, x, y, output, n):
    # y = a * x + y  (SAXPY: Single-precision A*X Plus Y)
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        output[idx] = a * x[idx] + y[idx]
    # FLOPS: 1 multiply + 1 add = 2 FLOPS per element
    # Bytes: read x[idx] (4B) + read y[idx] (4B) + write output[idx] (4B) = 12 B
    # AI = 2/12 = 0.167 FLOPS/byte
    # On T4: max perf = 0.167 * 320 GB/s = 53 GFLOPS
    # That's only 0.6% of peak compute (8100 GFLOPS)
    # -> This kernel can NEVER be compute-bound. Focus on memory!
```

## Measuring Whether You Are Memory-Bound or Compute-Bound

The roofline tells you the *ceiling*; measurement tells you where on the roofline you actually sit. The recipe is six lines of arithmetic against your timing:

```python
achieved_bw_gb_s    = total_bytes_moved / (time_s) / 1e9
achieved_gflops     = total_flops      / (time_s) / 1e9
bw_pct_of_peak      = achieved_bw_gb_s / peak_bw_gb_s   * 100
compute_pct_of_peak = achieved_gflops  / peak_gflops    * 100
ai = total_flops / total_bytes_moved
# Rule of thumb on a T4 (peak_bw ~ 320 GB/s, peak_gflops ~ 8100):
#   bw_pct >= 70%      -> you are memory-bound and saturating bandwidth (good)
#   compute_pct >= 70% -> you are compute-bound and saturating FLOPS  (good)
#   neither >= 70%     -> third bottleneck: latency, divergence, or occupancy
```

If neither is above ~70% you are not actually riding the roofline -- something else (kernel-launch overhead, warp stalls, low occupancy, divergent branches) is leaving compute units idle. That is your cue to drop into Nsight Compute and look at stall reasons rather than to tune coalescing further.

## Improving Arithmetic Intensity

The key strategies for improving AI (and thus moving from memory-bound to compute-bound):

1. **Data reuse through tiling**: Load data into shared memory once, use it multiple times. Matrix multiply uses this extensively.

2. **Kernel fusion**: Instead of running two kernels (read-compute-write, read-compute-write), fuse them into one (read-compute-compute-write). Eliminates the intermediate write and read.

```python
# BAD: Two separate kernels (low AI each)
@cuda.jit
def step1(a, b, temp, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        temp[idx] = a[idx] * b[idx]  # Write to global memory

@cuda.jit
def step2(temp, c, output, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        output[idx] = temp[idx] + c[idx]  # Read from global memory

# GOOD: Fused kernel (higher AI)
@cuda.jit
def fused(a, b, c, output, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        output[idx] = a[idx] * b[idx] + c[idx]  # No intermediate storage
```

### Worked example: scale + add + relu

Consider three element-wise ops applied in sequence to an array of size N (float32):

- `t1 = scalar * x`        -- 1 FLOP, 12 bytes (read x, write t1; x ignored thereafter)
- `t2 = t1 + b`            -- 1 FLOP, 12 bytes (read t1, read b, write t2)
- `y  = max(t2, 0)`        -- 1 FLOP, 8  bytes (read t2, write y)

Three separate kernels move **32 bytes per element** for **3 FLOPs** -- AI = 3/32 = 0.094.

Fused into one kernel: `y = max(scalar*x + b, 0)`. Each thread reads `x` and `b` once and writes `y` once: **12 bytes per element** for **3 FLOPs** -- AI = 0.25.

Total bytes per element drop from 32 -> 12, a **2.7x bandwidth saving**. On a memory-bound kernel that translates almost directly into a 2-3x speedup. Fusion is the #1 optimization in modern deep-learning compilers (TorchInductor, XLA, Triton) for exactly this reason.

3. **Loop within kernel**: Process multiple elements per thread to increase the compute-to-load ratio.

@cuda.jit
def vector_add(a, b, c, n):

    if idx < n:

@cuda.jit

    if idx < n:
        out[idx] = scalar * x[idx] + y[idx]

@cuda.jit
def heavy_compute(a, b, out, n):
    """Many FLOPs per element (high AI)."""
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        val = a[idx]
        for _ in range(100):  # 100 iterations of 2 FLOPs = 200 FLOPs
            val = val * b[idx] + a[idx]  # FMA (1 multiply + 1 add)
        out[idx] = val

def bench(kernel, args, grid, block, warmup=10, iters=50):
    for _ in range(warmup):

        kernel[grid, block](*args)

bytes_moved = n * 12

print(f"{'Vector add':<20} {t:<12.4f} {ai:<10.3f} {gflops:<10.2f} {eff_bw:<15.1f} {'Memory'}")

# SAXPY: 2 FLOPS, 12 bytes

# Heavy compute: ~200 FLOPS, 12 bytes
t = bench(heavy_compute, (a, b, c, n), BPG, TPB)

bytes_moved = n * 12

eff_bw = bytes_moved / (t / 1000) / 1e9

print(f"{'Heavy compute':<20} {t:<12.4f} {ai:<10.3f} {gflops:<10.2f} {eff_bw:<15.1f} {'Compute'}")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Demo 3: Kernel fusion benefit
# ------------------------------------------------------------------
@cuda.jit
def step1(a, b, temp, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        temp[idx] = a[idx] * b[idx]

@cuda.jit
def step2(temp, b, out, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        out[idx] = temp[idx] + b[idx]

@cuda.jit
def fused(a, b, out, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        out[idx] = a[idx] * b[idx] + b[idx]

temp = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

# Two-kernel approach
def bench_two_kernels():
    step1[BPG, TPB](a, b, temp, n)
    step2[BPG, TPB](temp, b, c, n)

for _ in range(10):
    bench_two_kernels()
cuda.synchronize()
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(50):
    bench_two_kernels()
cuda.synchronize()
t_two = (time.perf_counter() - start) / 50 * 1000

t_fused = bench(fused, (a, b, c, n), BPG, TPB)

print(f"\n=== Kernel Fusion Benefit ===")
print(f"Two separate kernels: {t_two:.4f} ms")
print(f"Fused kernel:         {t_fused:.4f} ms")
print(f"Speedup from fusion:  {t_two / t_fused:.2f}x")
print(f"(Fusion eliminates intermediate global memory write + read)")

sections.title: Roofline vs Reality
sections.content:
Real kernels rarely hit the roofline. Common reasons for the gap: - **Uncoalesced memory access**: Wastes bandwidth, reducing effective memory throughput - **Warp divergence**: Wastes compute cycles, reducing effective FLOPS - **Low occupancy**: Cannot fully hide latency, leaving execution units idle - **Instruction overhead**: Index calculations, boundary checks, etc. consume cycles without useful FLOPS - **Bank conflicts**: Shared memory bank conflicts serialize parallel accesses The optimization process is iterative: measure, identify the bottleneck, fix it, repeat. ## Theoretical vs Achieved Occupancy The occupancy calculator (Module 5) reports a **theoretical** number based on register count, shared memory, and block size. The runtime **achieved** occupancy is what actually matters and is typically lower because of three effects: (1) blocks finish at different times, so toward the end of a kernel many SMs run partial waves ("tail effect"), (2) blocks within a wave may diverge in execution time so warps sit idle waiting for their block to finish, and (3) launch configuration may not divide evenly into the GPU's SM count, leaving some SMs underutilized in the final wave. The gap is what Nsight Compute reports as `sm__warps_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active`. If theoretical says 75% but achieved says 35%, you are losing performance not to occupancy *limits* but to *imbalance*. The fix is usually grid-stride loops, larger problem sizes (so the tail wave is a small fraction of the total), or persistent kernels. ## Connecting It All: The Optimization Decision Tree Now you can combine everything from the entire course into a single decision framework: ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Step 1: Calculate AI
Plot on roofline"] D{"AI vs Ridge Point
25.3 FLOP/byte"} M["MEMORY-BOUND
(AI < 25.3)"] C["COMPUTE-BOUND
(AI > 25.3)"] MFix["Fix coalescing (Sec 1-4)
Fix data layout
Use tiling (Module 4)
Fuse kernels"] CFix["Fix divergence (Module 5)
Fix occupancy (Module 5)
Reduce instructions
Use FMA operations"] Start --> D D -->|"<"| M --> MFix D -->|">"| C --> CFix classDef mem fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca classDef cmp fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#dbeafe class M,MFix mem class C,CFix cmp ``` > **What would happen if...** you spent a week optimizing divergence in a memory-bound kernel? Nothing. The roofline model shows your kernel cannot exceed AI * Bandwidth, regardless of how perfect your compute utilization is. Always identify the bottleneck before optimizing. > **Gotcha:** Students often see their kernel achieving 50 GFLOPS and think "that is only 0.6% of T4 peak, my kernel must be terrible." But if the kernel is vector addition (AI = 0.083), the theoretical max is only ~27 GFLOPS. Achieving 50 GFLOPS would actually mean you are exceeding the roofline -- likely because of L2 cache hits reducing effective memory traffic. Always compare against the roofline, not peak compute. ## Key Takeaways 1. **Arithmetic intensity (AI) = FLOPS / Bytes** -- determines whether your kernel is memory-bound or compute-bound 2. **Most GPU kernels are memory-bound** -- vector operations, reductions, and stencils all have low AI 3. **The roofline model** visually shows max achievable performance given AI and hardware specs 4. **Memory-bound kernels**: optimize coalescing (sections 1-4), caching, data layout, and tiling (Module 4) 5. **Compute-bound kernels**: optimize instruction mix, occupancy (Module 5), and divergence (Module 5) 6. **Kernel fusion and tiling** are the primary ways to increase AI 7. **You now have all the tools** to analyze any CUDA kernel: thread model (Module 2), shared memory (Module 3), tiling (Module 4), warp behavior (Module 5), and performance analysis (this module)

sections.title: Scientific Method for GPU Tuning
sections.content:
Optimizing GPU code without measurement is like navigating without a compass -- you might get lucky, but you will probably go in circles. This section brings together every technique from the course into a systematic optimization methodology.

The profiling-driven optimization loop is:

1. **Measure** current performance (time, bandwidth, FLOPS)
2. **Identify** the bottleneck using the roofline model (sections 5-8)
3. **Hypothesize** a fix based on what you learned:
   - Memory-bound? Fix coalescing (sections 1-4), add tiling (Module 4), fuse kernels
   - Compute-bound? Fix divergence (Module 5), improve occupancy (Module 5)
4. **Apply** the fix
5. **Measure** again to verify improvement
6. **Repeat** until satisfied

This is the scientific method applied to performance engineering. Never skip steps 1 and 5 -- they are the most important.

## Timing GPU Kernels: Python-side vs CUDA Events

Python's `time.perf_counter()` measures wall-clock time, which includes Python overhead and is subject to OS scheduling noise. As long as you bracket the timed region with `cuda.synchronize()` it is good enough to compare kernel versions. For tighter measurements -- especially when timing a single launch or measuring just the GPU portion of a pipeline -- CUDA **events** are recorded directly on the GPU stream and are immune to host-side jitter.

```python
import time
from numba import cuda

# Method 1: Python-side timing with synchronization
def time_python(kernel, args, grid, block, iters=100):
    for _ in range(10):                     # warmup
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    return (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters * 1000  # ms

# Method 2: CUDA events (GPU-side timing, more precise)
def time_events(kernel, args, grid, block, iters=100):
    for _ in range(10):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    start_evt = cuda.event()
    end_evt   = cuda.event()
    start_evt.record()
    for _ in range(iters):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    end_evt.record()
    end_evt.synchronize()
    return cuda.event_elapsed_time(start_evt, end_evt) / iters  # ms
```

For in-course exercises Python-side timing is fine. When you are chasing the last 10% of performance, switch to events.

### Why Warmup Matters

The first kernel launch is always slow due to:
- **JIT compilation**: Numba compiles CUDA kernels on first invocation
- **Context initialization**: CUDA context setup on first GPU operation
- **Memory allocation**: First `device_array` or `to_device` triggers GPU memory setup
- **Cache cold-start**: L2 cache is empty on first access

Always run 5-10 warmup iterations before timing. The first call may include 100-500 ms of JIT compilation while subsequent calls run in fractions of a millisecond -- the ratio can be **1000x**, completely invalidating your measurements.

sections.title: Benchmark Harness
sections.content:
A robust benchmark function should:

1. Accept any kernel with its arguments
2. Perform warmup iterations
3. Synchronize before and after the timed region
4. Run enough iterations to average out noise
5. Report multiple statistics (mean, min, max, std)

```python
def benchmark_kernel(kernel, grid, block, args, warmup=10, iters=100):
    """Robust GPU kernel benchmarking."""
    # Warmup (includes JIT compilation on first call)
    for _ in range(warmup):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    
    # Timed iterations
    times = []
    for _ in range(iters):
        cuda.synchronize()
        start = time.perf_counter()
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
        cuda.synchronize()
        end = time.perf_counter()
        times.append((end - start) * 1000)  # ms
    
    times = np.array(times)
    return {
        'mean': np.mean(times),
        'std': np.std(times),
        'min': np.min(times),
        'max': np.max(times),
        'median': np.median(times)
    }
```

from numba import cuda
import numpy as np
import math
import time

# ==================================================================
# Complete profiling & optimization example
# Task: compute output[i] = sqrt(a[i]^2 + b[i]^2)
# ==================================================================

# --- Benchmark Harness ---
def benchmark(kernel, grid, block, args, warmup=10, iters=50):
    for _ in range(warmup):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    times = []
    for _ in range(iters):
        cuda.synchronize()
        start = time.perf_counter()
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
        cuda.synchronize()
        t = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        times.append(t)
    times = np.array(times)
    return {'mean': np.mean(times), 'std': np.std(times),
            'min': np.min(times), 'median': np.median(times)}

def analyze(label, time_ms, n, flops_per_elem, bytes_per_elem):
    total_flops = n * flops_per_elem
    total_bytes = n * bytes_per_elem
    gflops = total_flops / (time_ms / 1000) / 1e9
    bw = total_bytes / (time_ms / 1000) / 1e9
    ai = flops_per_elem / bytes_per_elem
    print(f"{label:<24} {time_ms:>8.4f} ms | {bw:>7.1f} GB/s | {gflops:>7.1f} GFLOPS | AI={ai:.2f}")
    return time_ms

# --- Kernel versions ---
@cuda.jit
def magnitude_v1(a, b, output, n):
    """Naive: uses ** operator."""
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        output[idx] = math.sqrt(a[idx] ** 2 + b[idx] ** 2)

@cuda.jit
def magnitude_v2(a, b, output, n):
    """Better: uses multiply instead of pow."""
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        ai = a[idx]
        bi = b[idx]
        output[idx] = math.sqrt(ai * ai + bi * bi)

@cuda.jit
def magnitude_v3(a, b, output, n):
    """Best: each thread strides by gridDim*blockDim for coalesced multi-element work."""
    tid    = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    stride = cuda.blockDim.x * cuda.gridDim.x
    # Grid-stride loop -- consecutive threads still hit consecutive
    # addresses on every iteration (warp [tid..tid+31] -> [a[tid]..a[tid+31]],
    # then [a[tid+stride]..a[tid+stride+31]], etc). COALESCED.
    for idx in range(tid, n, stride):
        ai = a[idx]
        bi = b[idx]
        output[idx] = math.sqrt(ai * ai + bi * bi)

# --- Setup ---
n = 8_000_000
TPB = 256
BPG = math.ceil(n / TPB)
# For V3 we deliberately launch FEWER blocks so each thread does ~4 elements
BPG_v3 = math.ceil(n / (TPB * 4))

a = cuda.to_device(np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32))
b = cuda.to_device(np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32))
out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

print("=== GPU Kernel Optimization: Step by Step ===")
print(f"Problem: output[i] = sqrt(a[i]^2 + b[i]^2), N = {n:,}")
print(f"FLOPS/elem: 4 (mul, mul, add, sqrt), Bytes/elem: 12 (2 reads + 1 write)")
print(f"Arithmetic intensity: {4/12:.2f} FLOPS/byte (memory-bound!)\n")
print(f"{'Version':<24} {'Time':>12} | {'BW':>9} | {'Compute':>11} | {'AI'}")
print("-" * 75)

results = []
r1 = benchmark(magnitude_v1, BPG, TPB, (a, b, out, n))
t1 = analyze("V1: Naive (x**2)", r1['mean'], n, 4, 12)
results.append(('V1', t1))

# Verify correctness
out_host = out.copy_to_host()
a_host = a.copy_to_host()
b_host = b.copy_to_host()
expected = np.sqrt(a_host**2 + b_host**2)
assert np.allclose(out_host, expected, atol=1e-5), "V1 incorrect!"

r2 = benchmark(magnitude_v2, BPG, TPB, (a, b, out, n))
t2 = analyze("V2: x*x instead", r2['mean'], n, 4, 12)
results.append(('V2', t2))

r3 = benchmark(magnitude_v3, BPG_v3, TPB, (a, b, out, n))
t3 = analyze("V3: grid-stride loop", r3['mean'], n, 4, 12)
results.append(('V3', t3))

# Verify V3
out_host = out.copy_to_host()
assert np.allclose(out_host, expected, atol=1e-5), "V3 incorrect!"

# NumPy reference
cuda.synchronize()
start = time.perf_counter()
np_result = np.sqrt(a_host**2 + b_host**2)
t_np = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
print(f"{'NumPy (CPU)':<24} {t_np:>8.4f} ms |")

print(f"\n=== Improvement Summary ===")
for name, t in results:
    print(f"{name}: {t:.4f} ms ({t1/t:.2f}x vs V1, {t_np/t:.1f}x vs NumPy)")

# --- Demonstrate the importance of warmup ---
print("\n=== Why Warmup Matters ===")

@cuda.jit
def fresh_kernel(a, out, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        out[idx] = a[idx] * 2.0

# First call includes JIT compilation
cuda.synchronize()
start = time.perf_counter()
fresh_kernel[BPG, TPB](a, out, n)
cuda.synchronize()
t_first = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000

# Second call is warmed up
cuda.synchronize()
start = time.perf_counter()
fresh_kernel[BPG, TPB](a, out, n)
cuda.synchronize()
t_second = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000

print(f"First call (includes JIT):  {t_first:.2f} ms")
print(f"Second call (warmed up):    {t_second:.4f} ms")
print(f"Ratio: {t_first/t_second:.0f}x -- THIS is why warmup is essential!")

sections.title: Profiling with Nsight Compute
sections.content:
Python-side timing tells you *how long* a kernel takes; **Nsight Compute** (`ncu`) tells you *why*. It is the canonical kernel-level GPU profiler in 2026 and the tool every CUDA performance engineer reaches for first. The information it surfaces -- achieved bandwidth as a fraction of peak, sector-level memory efficiency, warp stall reasons, achieved occupancy, instruction mix -- is exactly what you need to land your kernel on the roofline.

## Running ncu on a numba kernel

Numba kernels compile to standard CUDA modules, so `ncu` profiles them like any other CUDA program. From a Colab cell or a shell:

```bash
# Profile a Python script that launches numba kernels
ncu --set full --target-processes all -o report python my_kernel.py

# Or use the lighter-weight basic set for a quick look
ncu --set basic python my_kernel.py

# Limit profiling to one specific kernel (otherwise ncu profiles every launch)
ncu --kernel-name regex:magnitude_v3 --launch-skip 10 --launch-count 1 python my_kernel.py
```

The `--launch-skip 10` is important -- it skips warmup launches (including JIT) and profiles a representative iteration.

## The four sections you actually read

A full ncu report has dozens of sections. In practice, look at these four in order:

1. **GPU Speed Of Light** -- top-line summary. Two numbers matter: `Memory Throughput %` and `Compute (SM) Throughput %`. Whichever is higher tells you the bottleneck. If both are below 60% you have a *third* problem (latency, divergence, occupancy) that no amount of memory or compute tuning will fix.

2. **Memory Workload Analysis** -- sector-level memory efficiency. The key metric is `L1/TEX Cache | Sector Hit Rate` plus the global memory `Sectors / Request` ratio. A coalesced kernel reading float32 hits 1.0 sectors/request; an uncoalesced kernel can hit 8 or higher, directly indicating wasted bandwidth.

3. **Compute Workload Analysis** -- breaks down which pipelines are busy (FMA, ALU, special function, tensor). Useful for spotting that you are bottlenecked on transcendentals (`log`, `sqrt`, `exp`) rather than FMAs.

4. **Warp State (Scheduler) Statistics** -- the **stall reasons**. Common ones: `Stall Long Scoreboard` (waiting for global memory -- you are memory-bound), `Stall Short Scoreboard` (waiting for shared memory -- bank conflicts or just lots of shared memory traffic), `Stall Wait` (synchronization), `Stall Barrier` (`__syncthreads()`), `Stall MIO Throttle` (special function unit saturation). The dominant stall reason directly names your bottleneck.

## Achieved vs requested bandwidth

Ncu reports both `Memory Throughput` (achieved bytes/sec across the DRAM bus, including waste) and effective metrics that separate *requested* bytes (what the kernel asked for) from *transferred* bytes (what actually moved through DRAM). The ratio requested / transferred is your **memory access efficiency**: 1.0 means perfect coalescing, 0.125 (=1/8) means you are wasting 87% of every transaction. This single number is more diagnostic than any single timing measurement.

## When ncu is not available

In Colab the GPU may not expose performance counters and `ncu` will refuse to collect detailed metrics. In that case you can still get useful signals: (a) compute achieved bandwidth from your benchmark (the recipe from section 7), (b) compare against the roofline ceiling for that AI, and (c) inspect the SASS via `cuda.compile_ptx_for_current_device()` to confirm the compiler emitted what you expect (FMAs vs separate mul+add, vectorized loads, etc.). On real hardware always rerun under ncu before declaring optimization complete.

sections.title: Identifying Bottlenecks
sections.content:
Once you have timing data, the next step is determining **why** your kernel is slow. Here is a systematic approach. ### Step 1: Calculate Achieved Bandwidth and FLOPS ```python def analyze_kernel(time_ms, data_bytes, flops): """Determine if kernel is memory-bound or compute-bound.""" achieved_bw = data_bytes / (time_ms / 1000) / 1e9 # GB/s achieved_gflops = flops / (time_ms / 1000) / 1e9 # GFLOPS ai = flops / data_bytes # FLOPS/byte # T4 specs peak_bw = 320 # GB/s peak_gflops = 8100 # GFLOPS FP32 bw_utilization = achieved_bw / peak_bw * 100 compute_utilization = achieved_gflops / peak_gflops * 100 print(f"Achieved bandwidth: {achieved_bw:.1f} GB/s ({bw_utilization:.1f}% of peak)") print(f"Achieved compute: {achieved_gflops:.1f} GFLOPS ({compute_utilization:.1f}% of peak)") print(f"Arithmetic intensity: {ai:.2f} FLOPS/byte") if bw_utilization > compute_utilization: print("Diagnosis: MEMORY-BOUND (bandwidth is the bottleneck)") else: print("Diagnosis: COMPUTE-BOUND (compute is the bottleneck)") ``` ### Step 2: Check Memory Access Efficiency If memory-bound, check whether you are achieving reasonable bandwidth: | Achieved BW | Diagnosis | Action | |------------|-----------|--------| | >80% of peak | Excellent; little room for improvement | Focus on algorithm changes | | 50-80% of peak | Good; some optimization possible | Check coalescing, consider tiling | | 20-50% of peak | Poor; likely uncoalesced access | Fix memory access patterns | | <20% of peak | Very poor; severe access pattern issues | Restructure data layout entirely | ### Step 3: Check Compute Efficiency If compute-bound, check whether you are achieving reasonable FLOPS: | Achieved FLOPS | Diagnosis | Action | |---------------|-----------|--------| | >50% of peak | Excellent | Minor tuning only | | 25-50% of peak | Good | Check occupancy, divergence | | <25% of peak | Poor | Major optimization needed | ## What to Check First When Slow: Decision Tree When a kernel is unexpectedly slow, work through these questions **in order**. Each step has a tool that answers it. ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Kernel is too slow.
Where do I start?"] Q1{"1. Launch config sane?
(grid x block >= ~10x #SMs?
block multiple of 32?)"} Q2{"2. Memory coalesced?
(ncu: Sectors/Request ~1.0?)"} Q3{"3. Achieved BW vs peak?"} Q4{"4. Dominant stall reason?
(ncu Warp State)"} Q5{"5. Achieved occupancy
vs theoretical?"} Q6{"6. Divergence?
(ncu: warp execution efficiency)"} Q7{"7. Register pressure?
(--ptxas-options=-v)"} Done["You are within 80% of roofline.
Stop."] Fix1["Fix block/grid:
TPB=128-256, BPG large enough"] Fix2["Reorder accesses,
use SoA, tile via shmem"] Fix3[">70%: memory-bound (good).
Increase AI via tiling or fusion."] Fix4["Long Scoreboard -> mem-bound
Short Scoreboard -> bank conflicts
Barrier -> too many syncs"] Fix5["Achieved << theoretical:
tail effect, imbalanced blocks.
Use grid-stride loops."] Fix6["Refactor branches per Module 5"] Fix7["Reduce locals,
or accept lower occupancy"] S --> Q1 -->|no| Fix1 Q1 -->|yes| Q2 -->|no| Fix2 Q2 -->|yes| Q3 -->|<70%| Q4 Q3 -->|>=70%| Fix3 Q4 --> Fix4 Fix4 --> Q5 -->|gap| Fix5 Q5 -->|matches| Q6 -->|yes| Fix6 Q6 -->|no| Q7 -->|high| Fix7 Q7 -->|low| Done ``` The most common path: question 1 reveals the launch config is fine, question 2 reveals uncoalesced access, fixing it gets you to 70%+ of peak bandwidth, and you stop. Walking down the tree saves you from spending a week tuning occupancy on a kernel whose real problem was a swapped index.

sections.title: The Optimization Checklist
sections.content:
When your kernel is too slow, work through this checklist **in order**. Each item addresses the most common performance issues, from most impactful to least.

### Priority 1: Memory Access Patterns
- [ ] Are global memory accesses coalesced? (Consecutive threads access consecutive addresses)
- [ ] Is the data layout SoA rather than AoS?
- [ ] Can you use shared memory to stage data for non-coalesced algorithms?
- [ ] Are you reading/writing only the data you need? (No unnecessary loads)
- [ ] Can you use vectorized loads (e.g. `float2`/`float4` via reinterpreted views) to cut transaction count in half?

### Priority 2: Parallelism
- [ ] Are you launching enough thread blocks to saturate all SMs? (Rule of thumb: at least 10x the SM count.)
- [ ] Is the block size a multiple of 32 (ideally 128-256)?
- [ ] Is occupancy at least 50%? (Check register and shared memory usage.) Compare *achieved* vs *theoretical* occupancy in ncu -- a large gap means tail effects, not occupancy *limits*.
- [ ] Can you increase the grid size by having each thread process one element? Or use a grid-stride loop so each thread processes several?

### Priority 3: Instruction Efficiency
- [ ] Is warp divergence minimized? (Branch-free where possible)
- [ ] Can expensive operations be replaced? (division -> multiplication, `pow` -> repeated multiply, `**2` -> `x*x`)
- [ ] Can you use FMA (fused multiply-add) instructions?
- [ ] Are loop bounds known at compile time? (Enables loop unrolling.)

### Priority 4: Kernel Launch Overhead
- [ ] Can multiple kernels be fused into one? (See section 7 for the bytes-saved calculation.)
- [ ] Is the problem large enough to amortize launch overhead? (~5-10 microseconds per launch)
- [ ] Are you doing unnecessary CPU-GPU synchronization between kernels?

### Priority 5: Data Transfer
- [ ] Are you minimizing CPU-GPU data transfers?
- [ ] Can you keep data on the GPU between kernel launches?
- [ ] Are you using pinned (page-locked) memory for necessary transfers?

## Antipatterns Gallery

A quick reference for the things that most often kill performance:

1. **Implicit copies in inner loops** -- writing `arr = some_device_array.copy_to_host()` inside a Python loop triggers a full DtoH transfer per iteration. Move outside the loop.
2. **`(idx * stride) % n` style indexing** -- deliberately or accidentally producing strided access. Reorder threads, not data.
3. **Double-loading the same value** -- `a[idx] * 2 + a[idx]` loads `a[idx]` twice from global memory. Cache in a local: `ai = a[idx]; out = ai * 2 + ai`.
4. **Dependent atomic chains** -- `atomicAdd(&hist[a[i]], 1)` where many threads target the same bin serializes through the atomic. Use shared-memory-staged histograms (Module 10).
5. **Redundant `cuda.syncthreads()`** -- one sync at the end of a phase suffices. Per-iteration syncs inside a tight loop kill warp scheduling latitude.
6. **`x ** 2`** -- compiles to a `pow` call with a runtime exponent test. Always write `x * x`.

sections.title: Complete Example & Roadmap
sections.content:
## A Complete Optimization Example Let us walk through optimizing a real kernel step by step. **Task**: Compute element-wise `output[i] = sqrt(a[i]^2 + b[i]^2)` for large arrays. **Version 1 (Naive)**: ```python @cuda.jit def magnitude_v1(a, b, output, n): idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x if idx < n: output[idx] = math.sqrt(a[idx] ** 2 + b[idx] ** 2) ``` **Version 2 (Avoid pow, use multiply)**: ```python @cuda.jit def magnitude_v2(a, b, output, n): idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x if idx < n: ai = a[idx] bi = b[idx] output[idx] = math.sqrt(ai * ai + bi * bi) # multiply faster than **2 ``` **Version 3 (Grid-stride loop -- multiple elements per thread, still coalesced)**: ```python @cuda.jit def magnitude_v3(a, b, output, n): tid = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x stride = cuda.blockDim.x * cuda.gridDim.x # On each iteration, warp reads a[tid..tid+31] -- still coalesced. for idx in range(tid, n, stride): ai = a[idx] bi = b[idx] output[idx] = math.sqrt(ai * ai + bi * bi) ``` Each version addresses a different aspect: V1 is baseline, V2 reduces instruction cost, V3 amortizes launch overhead and increases instruction-level parallelism. Crucially, V3 uses the **grid-stride loop** pattern: thread `tid` handles elements `tid, tid+stride, tid+2*stride, ...` where `stride = blockDim.x * gridDim.x`. On every iteration, the 32 threads of a warp still hit 32 consecutive addresses, so coalescing is preserved. > **Coalescing antipattern -- avoid this:** A tempting but **wrong** way to give each thread multiple elements is `base = tid * K; for i in range(K): out[base + i] = ...`. With K=4, warp 0 reads positions [0..3, 4..7, 8..11, ...] -- stride-4 within each warp, **not coalesced**. Always use the grid-stride pattern (`for idx in range(tid, n, stride)`), not the contiguous-per-thread pattern. ## Real Error Messages and Profiling Pitfalls Here are actual errors you will encounter when profiling and optimizing: ``` # Error 1: Forgetting cuda.synchronize() before timing # Result: Your timing includes only kernel LAUNCH overhead (~5us), # not actual kernel execution. You think your kernel runs in 0.005ms # when it actually takes 2.5ms. # FIX: Always call cuda.synchronize() before and after the timed region. # Error 2: Timing includes JIT compilation numba.cuda.cudadrv.driver.CudaAPIError: [1] Call to cuLaunchKernel results in CUDA_ERROR_INVALID_VALUE # This often appears on first launch with wrong grid/block dimensions. # Or: first launch takes 200ms, second takes 0.2ms. # FIX: Always warmup 5-10 iterations before timing. # Error 3: Out of GPU memory during benchmarking numba.cuda.cudadrv.driver.CudaAPIError: [2] Call to cuMemAlloc results in CUDA_ERROR_OUT_OF_MEMORY # You allocated many arrays and never freed them. # FIX: Use del d_array; cuda.current_context().deallocations.clear() # between benchmark runs, or reuse arrays. # Error 4: Misleading results from CPU-GPU transfers # Your benchmark includes cuda.to_device() in the timed region, # making the GPU look slow. Kernel time is fast, but transfer dominates. # FIX: Move all to_device() calls BEFORE the timed region. ``` ## The Complete Optimization Roadmap: From Module 2 to Here You now have all the tools to analyze and optimize any CUDA kernel: ```mermaid flowchart TB M2["Module 2: Thread Model
Grid/block sizing
1 thread per output element"] M3["Module 3: Shared Memory
Data sharing within block
Enables tiling"] M4["Module 4: Tiled Matmul
Reduces global traffic by TILE_SIZE
Most important algorithm"] M5["Module 5: Warps/Execution
SIMT, divergence, occupancy
WHY patterns are fast/slow"] M6["Module 6: Performance
Coalescing + Roofline + Profiling"] M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 --> M6 Q["FOR ANY KERNEL ASK:
1. Correct? (M2)
2. Uses shared memory? (M3)
3. Reuses data via tiling? (M4)
4. Block size mult of 32, good occupancy? (M5)
5. Coalesced accesses? (M6 sec 1-4)
6. What does roofline say? (M6 sec 5-8)
7. Measured properly? (M6 sec 9+)"] M6 --> Q ``` ## Anti-Patterns: What NOT to Do 1. **Don't optimize without measuring** -- you will waste time on non-bottlenecks (the roofline model from sections 5-8 tells you where to focus) 2. **Don't chase 100% occupancy** -- 50-75% is usually sufficient (Module 5) 3. **Don't add complexity for marginal gains** -- a 2% improvement is not worth 100 lines of code 4. **Don't compare GPU time to unoptimized CPU code** -- always compare against numpy/optimized CPU 5. **Don't forget warmup** -- JIT compilation will dominate your first measurement > **What would happen if...** you applied every optimization from this course to a simple vector addition kernel? Coalescing, optimal block size, high occupancy. You would reach ~280 GB/s effective bandwidth on a T4 (87% of peak). But you could never exceed 0.083 * 320 ~ 27 GFLOPS. The kernel is fundamentally memory-bound. The only way to make vector addition faster is to do it with FEWER memory operations -- for example, by fusing it with the next operation in your pipeline. ## Key Takeaways 1. **Always measure before and after** every optimization 2. **Use proper warmup and synchronization** for accurate timing 3. **Calculate achieved bandwidth and FLOPS** to identify the bottleneck type (use the roofline model) 4. **Follow the optimization checklist** in priority order 5. **Reach for Nsight Compute** when you need stall reasons or sector-level memory efficiency 6. **Stop when satisfied** -- diminishing returns are real 7. **You now have a complete toolkit**: thread model, shared memory, tiling, warp understanding, coalescing, roofline analysis, and profiling methodology

references.title: CUDA C Best Practices: Performance Metrics

quiz.question: What is memory coalescing?
options: Combining multiple GPU cores into a single processing unit
The hardware combining memory requests from threads in a warp into fewer transactions
Merging two separate arrays into one for faster access
Caching frequently accessed memory locations in registers
quiz.explanation: Memory coalescing is when the GPU hardware combines memory access requests from multiple threads in a warp into a minimal number of memory transactions. When consecutive threads access consecutive memory addresses, a single 128-byte transaction can serve all 32 threads, instead of requiring up to 32 separate transactions.

quiz.question: A 2D matrix is stored in row-major order. Which access pattern is coalesced?
options: Consecutive threads reading consecutive rows (same column)
Consecutive threads reading consecutive columns (same row)
Each thread reading a random element
All threads reading the same element
quiz.explanation: In row-major storage, elements within the same row are consecutive in memory. When consecutive threads read consecutive columns (along a row), they access consecutive memory addresses, producing coalesced access. Reading consecutive rows accesses addresses that are an entire row apart -- severely uncoalesced.

quiz.question: A vector addition kernel (c[i] = a[i] + b[i]) has an arithmetic intensity of approximately 0.083 FLOPS/byte. On a T4 GPU (320 GB/s, 8.1 TFLOPS), this kernel is:
options: Compute-bound: limited by the GPU's FLOPS
Memory-bound: limited by memory bandwidth
Balanced: equally limited by compute and memory
Latency-bound: limited by kernel launch overhead
quiz.explanation: The T4's ridge point is 8100/320 = 25.3 FLOPS/byte. At 0.083 FLOPS/byte, vector addition is far below the ridge point, making it severely memory-bound. Maximum achievable compute is 0.083 * 320 ~ 27 GFLOPS, which is only 0.3% of the T4's peak. No amount of compute optimization will help -- only memory bandwidth optimization matters.

quiz.question: What is the primary benefit of kernel fusion (combining two separate kernels into one)?
options: It reduces register usage per thread
It eliminates intermediate global memory writes and reads between kernels
It allows using more shared memory
It increases warp-level parallelism
quiz.explanation: When two kernels run separately, the first writes intermediate results to global memory and the second reads them back. Fusing them into one kernel keeps intermediate values in registers, eliminating the expensive global memory round-trip. For memory-bound kernels, this can provide a 1.5-2x speedup.

quiz.question: Why is warmup essential when benchmarking CUDA kernels in numba?
options: The GPU needs time to reach operating temperature
CUDA contexts require 10 seconds to initialize
The first kernel launch includes JIT compilation overhead that can be 100-1000x the actual kernel time
Memory needs to be pre-fetched from disk to GPU
quiz.explanation: Numba uses Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation for CUDA kernels. The first call to a kernel triggers compilation from Python to PTX to GPU machine code, which can take 50-500ms. The actual kernel might run in 0.1ms. Without warmup, you measure compilation time, not kernel performance. Always run 5-10 warmup iterations before timing.

quiz.question: You measure your kernel achieving 45 GB/s on a T4 GPU (320 GB/s peak bandwidth). You confirmed the kernel is memory-bound. What is the most likely cause?
options: Low occupancy (not enough warps)
Warp divergence in branch statements
Uncoalesced memory access patterns
Too many kernel launches
quiz.explanation: At only 15% of peak bandwidth, the most likely cause is uncoalesced memory access. Uncoalesced patterns waste bandwidth by transferring much more data than needed (each warp might trigger many sector transactions instead of 1). Low occupancy and divergence would not typically reduce bandwidth utilization this severely.

quiz.question: Why is Structure of Arrays (SoA) preferred over Array of Structures (AoS) for GPU programming?
options: SoA uses less total memory
SoA allows more threads per block
SoA enables coalesced access when all threads read the same field, since field values are contiguous in memory
SoA is required by the CUDA programming model
quiz.explanation: In SoA, all x-values are contiguous, all y-values are contiguous, etc. When threads in a warp all read the x-field, they access consecutive addresses (coalesced). In AoS, x-values are interleaved with y and z values, so reading all x-values requires strided access (uncoalesced). SoA doesn't save memory -- it improves access patterns.

quiz.question: Your kernel achieves 80% of peak memory bandwidth. Which optimization should you try NEXT?
options: Improve coalescing -- there is still 20% bandwidth to recover
Increase arithmetic intensity through tiling or algorithmic changes, since memory access is already efficient
Increase occupancy to 100%
Reduce register usage
quiz.explanation: At 80% of peak bandwidth, your memory access patterns are already very efficient. Further coalescing improvements will yield diminishing returns. The right move is to increase arithmetic intensity -- use tiling/shared memory to reuse loaded data, or fuse operations to do more compute per byte loaded. This shifts the kernel toward compute-bound territory where additional compute is effectively 'free'.

exercise.title: Profile and Optimize a Poorly-Written Kernel
exercise.description:
You are given a deliberately poorly-written kernel that computes `output[i] = sqrt(a[i]^2 + b[i]^2) + log(abs(a[i]) + 1)` for large arrays. Your task is to profile it, identify the bottlenecks, and apply optimizations step by step.

**Requirements:**
1. Start with the provided naive kernel and measure its performance
2. Calculate achieved bandwidth and GFLOPS to determine if memory-bound or compute-bound
3. Apply at least 3 optimizations, measuring after each one:
   - Fix the uncoalesced access pattern (the naive version reads from a transposed-like layout)
   - Replace expensive operations (pow, redundant loads)
   - Try processing multiple elements per thread using a grid-stride loop (so each warp still hits 32 consecutive addresses per iteration)
4. Report a summary table showing time, bandwidth, and speedup for each version
5. Verify correctness by comparing all versions against numpy

**Hints:**
- The naive kernel loads data with stride -- fixing this alone may give 3-10x speedup
- `x ** 2` compiles to a pow() call; `x * x` is a simple multiply (much faster)
- Loading `a[idx]` twice generates two separate memory transactions -- cache it in a local variable
- For multi-element-per-thread, use the grid-stride loop pattern `for idx in range(tid, n, blockDim.x * gridDim.x)`. Do NOT use `base = tid * K; for i in range(K): ...` -- that produces stride-K access within a warp and is uncoalesced
- Use `cuda.synchronize()` before and after timing

exercise.solution:
!pip install numba

from numba import cuda
import numpy as np
import math
import time

# --- Version 1: Deliberately BAD kernel ---
@cuda.jit
def kernel_naive(a, b, output, n, stride):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        real_idx = (idx * stride) % n  # Strided = uncoalesced
        val_a_sq = a[real_idx] ** 2     # Slow pow()
        val_b_sq = b[real_idx] ** 2     # Slow pow()
        log_val = math.log(abs(a[real_idx]) + 1.0)  # Redundant load
        output[real_idx] = math.sqrt(val_a_sq + val_b_sq) + log_val

# --- Version 2: Fix coalescing (sequential access) ---
@cuda.jit
def kernel_v2(a, b, output, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        val_a_sq = a[idx] ** 2
        val_b_sq = b[idx] ** 2
        log_val = math.log(abs(a[idx]) + 1.0)
        output[idx] = math.sqrt(val_a_sq + val_b_sq) + log_val

# --- Version 3: Fix operations (multiply, cache loads) ---
@cuda.jit
def kernel_v3(a, b, output, n):
    idx = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    if idx < n:
        ai = a[idx]  # Load once, reuse
        bi = b[idx]
        output[idx] = math.sqrt(ai * ai + bi * bi) + math.log(abs(ai) + 1.0)

# --- Version 4: Multi-element per thread via GRID-STRIDE LOOP ---
# IMPORTANT: thread `tid` handles tid, tid+stride, tid+2*stride, ...
# where stride = blockDim.x * gridDim.x. On every iteration the warp
# still touches 32 consecutive addresses -> COALESCED.
#
# Anti-pattern (DO NOT DO):
#     base = tid * 4
#     for i in range(4):
#         out[base+i] = ...
# That gives warp 0 positions [0..3, 4..7, ...] -- stride-4 within the
# warp, uncoalesced.
@cuda.jit
def kernel_v4(a, b, output, n):
    tid    = cuda.threadIdx.x + cuda.blockIdx.x * cuda.blockDim.x
    stride = cuda.blockDim.x * cuda.gridDim.x
    for idx in range(tid, n, stride):
        ai = a[idx]
        bi = b[idx]
        output[idx] = math.sqrt(ai * ai + bi * bi) + math.log(abs(ai) + 1.0)

def benchmark(kernel, grid, block, args, warmup=10, iters=50):
    for _ in range(warmup):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(iters):
        kernel[grid, block](*args)
    cuda.synchronize()
    return (time.perf_counter() - start) / iters * 1000

# Setup
n = 4_000_000
TPB = 256
BPG = math.ceil(n / TPB)
# For V4 launch fewer blocks so each thread does ~4 elements via the grid-stride loop
BPG_v4 = math.ceil(n / (TPB * 4))

a_host = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
b_host = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
expected = np.sqrt(a_host**2 + b_host**2) + np.log(np.abs(a_host) + 1.0)

d_a = cuda.to_device(a_host)
d_b = cuda.to_device(b_host)
d_out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

bytes_per_elem = 12  # 2 reads + 1 write, float32
flops_per_elem = 6   # mul, mul, add, sqrt, abs+add, log

print(f"Problem: output[i] = sqrt(a[i]^2 + b[i]^2) + log(|a[i]| + 1)")
print(f"N = {n:,}, AI = {flops_per_elem/bytes_per_elem:.2f} FLOPS/byte\n")
print(f"{'Version':<30} {'Time (ms)':<12} {'BW (GB/s)':<12} {'GFLOPS':<10} {'Speedup'}")
print("-" * 76)

# V1: Naive (strided)
t1 = benchmark(kernel_naive, BPG, TPB, (d_a, d_b, d_out, n, 7))
bw1 = n * bytes_per_elem / (t1/1000) / 1e9
gf1 = n * flops_per_elem / (t1/1000) / 1e9
print(f"{'V1: Naive (strided+pow)':<30} {t1:<12.4f} {bw1:<12.1f} {gf1:<10.1f} {'1.00x'}")

out1 = d_out.copy_to_host()

# V2: Fixed coalescing
t2 = benchmark(kernel_v2, BPG, TPB, (d_a, d_b, d_out, n))
bw2 = n * bytes_per_elem / (t2/1000) / 1e9
gf2 = n * flops_per_elem / (t2/1000) / 1e9
print(f"{'V2: Coalesced access':<30} {t2:<12.4f} {bw2:<12.1f} {gf2:<10.1f} {t1/t2:.2f}x")
assert np.allclose(d_out.copy_to_host(), expected, atol=1e-4)

# V3: Fixed operations
t3 = benchmark(kernel_v3, BPG, TPB, (d_a, d_b, d_out, n))
bw3 = n * bytes_per_elem / (t3/1000) / 1e9
gf3 = n * flops_per_elem / (t3/1000) / 1e9
print(f"{'V3: x*x + cached loads':<30} {t3:<12.4f} {bw3:<12.1f} {gf3:<10.1f} {t1/t3:.2f}x")
assert np.allclose(d_out.copy_to_host(), expected, atol=1e-4)

# V4: Grid-stride loop, multiple elements per thread (still coalesced)
t4 = benchmark(kernel_v4, BPG_v4, TPB, (d_a, d_b, d_out, n))
bw4 = n * bytes_per_elem / (t4/1000) / 1e9
gf4 = n * flops_per_elem / (t4/1000) / 1e9
print(f"{'V4: grid-stride loop':<30} {t4:<12.4f} {bw4:<12.1f} {gf4:<10.1f} {t1/t4:.2f}x")
assert np.allclose(d_out.copy_to_host(), expected, atol=1e-4)

print(f"\nTotal speedup (V1 -> V4): {t1/t4:.1f}x")
print(f"Bandwidth improved: {bw1:.1f} -> {bw4:.1f} GB/s")
